_meta.purpose: Per-state foundation data dictionary for Phase A.2 state-vertical hub pages (full-house new-construction foundation pour focus — basement, slab-on-grade, crawl, pier-and-beam, or pile depending on dominant regional type). Differentiated from concrete vertical by scope: concrete vertical = 600 sq ft driveway / patio / slab pour, foundation vertical = full-house 2,000 sq ft new-construction foundation pour or repair.
_meta.sources: USDA-NRCS state frost depth maps; ASCE 7-22 Seismic Design Category by mapped Ss/S1; ASCE 7-22 wind speed maps; ACI 318-19 + ACI 332 Residential Code; Census / NAHB regional foundation-type surveys (basement dominant in Upper Midwest + Northeast, slab in Sun Belt, crawl in Southeast); state-specific expansive soil disclosures (CO Soil Disclosure Act, TX Black Prairie, OK red clay, MS Yazoo clay); IECC 2021 climate zone mapping; state contractor licensing boards verified by URL; BLS OEWS May 2024 (SOC 47-2061 Construction Laborers state means as foundation-work labor anchor); EPA radon zone 1/2/3 mapping; state hurricane code adoptions (FBC HVHZ, LA UCC, NC Coastal, SC Coastal); New Madrid Seismic Zone counties.

fields.frost_line_inches: USDA-NRCS design frost depth (inches) — drives footing depth and frost-protected shallow foundation options
fields.dominant_foundation_type: basement / slab-on-grade / crawl-space / pier-and-beam / pile-supported / mixed-basement-slab
fields.expansive_soil_risk: very-high / high / moderate / low / none — soil shrink-swell index driving foundation design
fields.seismic_design_category: A / B / C / D0 / D1 / D2 / E / F — ASCE 7-22 SDC (drives reinforcement schedule + footing tie-down)
fields.sulfate_exposure_class: S0 / S1 / S2 / S3 — ACI 318 sulfate exposure category (drives Type II or V cement)
fields.hurricane_tier: HVHZ / wind_high / wind_moderate / wind_low / none — coastal hurricane wind tier driving foundation tie-down + pile requirements
fields.water_table_risk: very-high / high / moderate / low / very-low — drives sump-pump, waterproofing membrane, and capillary-break design
fields.iecc_zone: IECC 2021 climate zone mapping — drives foundation insulation + capillary-break requirements
fields.radon_zone: 1 / 2 / 3 — EPA radon-potential zone (1 highest, 3 lowest) — drives sub-slab depressurization rough-in
fields.license_board_name: Issuing authority for residential foundation contractor licensing
fields.permit_typical: Typical permit threshold for residential new-construction foundation pour
fields.construction_laborer_wage_mean_hourly: BLS OEWS May 2024 state mean hourly wage for SOC 47-2061 Construction Laborers (USD)
fields.avg_foundation_low: Typical low end of full-house 2,000 sq ft new-construction foundation pour for the dominant regional foundation type (USD)
fields.avg_foundation_high: Typical high end (with hurricane tie-down, deep pile, post-tension, basement waterproofing membrane, or expansive-soil mitigation) (USD)
fields.distinctive_law: Single most distinctive state-specific foundation, soil disclosure, seismic, hurricane tie-down, or contractor licensing rule
fields.climate_concern: Primary climate, soil, seismic, or hydrologic driver of foundation design and waterproofing spec in this state

AL.name: Alabama
AL.license_board_name: Alabama Home Builders Licensure Board (HBLB) — residential foundation work above $10,000
AL.permit_typical: city or county building permit required for new-construction foundation; HBLB residential builder license required above $10,000 contract; Alabama Department of Environmental Management Stormwater NOI required on every site above 1 acre disturbance
AL.distinctive_law: Alabama Code §34-14A makes the Alabama Home Builders Licensure Board the only state body that licenses residential foundation contractors above the $10,000 threshold, and Alabama is one of the only Gulf states requiring an HBLB residential builder license to perform new-construction slab-on-grade pours plus a separate Limited Specialty Construction credential for foundation underpinning, pier-and-beam retrofits, and ICF wall pours — the ALDEM Stormwater NOI additionally adds a $300-$500 line item to every Alabama subdivision lot foundation pour above 1 acre disturbed area.
AL.climate_concern: Alabama's combination of red-clay subsoil across the Birmingham, Tuscaloosa, and Montgomery suburbs requires aggressive 4-to-6-inch crushed-stone capillary break under every slab pour to manage clay-driven foundation heave during seasonal moisture cycles, and Mobile-Baldwin coastal exposure to Gulf hurricane uplift drives Florida-style hurricane tie-down strap detailing on every coastal Alabama foundation pour south of I-10, with retarder admixtures standard during May-September high-humidity Gulf Coast pours.

AK.name: Alaska
AK.license_board_name: Alaska Department of Commerce — Construction Contractors Section (Residential Endorsement)
AK.permit_typical: borough building permit + Construction Contractor with Residential Endorsement required; Anchorage and Fairbanks Municipality require ASCE 7-22 SDC D2 seismic-detailed foundation plans on every residential pour
AK.distinctive_law: Alaska AS 18.65.082 requires every Alaska foundation contractor to hold a Construction Contractor with Residential Endorsement plus carry a $20,000 surety bond — the highest residential foundation bond floor in the country — and the State of Alaska Cold Climate Housing Research Center separately requires permafrost-disturbance review on every interior Alaska residential pour above the discontinuous permafrost line, with pile foundations driven to 25-40 ft below grade as the dominant residential foundation type to keep heated structures from thawing the permafrost layer.
AK.climate_concern: Alaska's combination of 100-inch frost depth in interior climate zones 7-8, discontinuous permafrost across Fairbanks-North Pole-Delta Junction, and -50°F winter low temperatures forces every interior Alaska residential foundation onto driven steel piles, drilled-pier sock foundations, or thermosyphon-protected piles to keep the heated building envelope from thawing the permafrost layer below — and Anchorage and Mat-Su Borough seismic design category D2 plus Aleutian Chain D2 design forces aggressive rebar tie schedules and continuous footing reinforcement on every coastal residential foundation pour statewide.

AZ.name: Arizona
AZ.license_board_name: Arizona Registrar of Contractors — license B-1 Residential General Contractor or B-3 Residential Remodeling
AZ.permit_typical: city or county building permit required for new-construction foundation pour; ROC B-1 specialty license required on contracts above $1,000; mandatory ASTM C150 Type II/V sulfate-resistant cement on every Phoenix-Tucson residential pour
AZ.distinctive_law: Arizona Registrar of Contractors §32-1121 sets one of the lowest residential contracting thresholds in the country at $1,000 — every Arizona foundation contractor exceeding that figure must hold a B-1 Residential General Contractor license, and Arizona is one of the only Mountain West states requiring mandatory ASTM C150 Type II or Type V sulfate-resistant cement on every residential foundation pour due to high alkaline soil sulfate exposure across the Phoenix and Tucson basins, plus the City of Phoenix Soils Lab requires a geotechnical investigation report on every Maricopa County residential foundation pour above 0.5 acre lot.
AZ.climate_concern: Arizona's combination of high alkaline soil sulfate exposure (S2 ACI 318 category) across the Phoenix, Tucson, and Mesa basins forces ASTM C150 Type II or Type V sulfate-resistant cement specification on every residential foundation pour to prevent sulfate-driven concrete deterioration, and Arizona's expansive caliche-clay subsoil across the Sonoran Desert requires 4-to-6-inch crushed-aggregate base plus geotextile membrane separation on every Phoenix and Tucson slab pour to prevent clay-driven slab heave during monsoon-season moisture cycles.

AR.name: Arkansas
AR.license_board_name: Arkansas Contractors Licensing Board — Residential Builder license (>$2,000)
AR.permit_typical: city or county building permit required for new-construction foundation pour; Residential Builder license required above $2,000 contract; New Madrid Seismic Zone Special Inspection required in 11 northeast Arkansas counties
AR.distinctive_law: Arkansas Code §17-25-101 sets one of the lowest residential contracting thresholds at $2,000 — and Arkansas is unusual among Southern states in adopting the New Madrid Seismic Zone Special Inspection Program through Arkansas Code §12-30 requiring continuous-rebar-tie footings, minimum 3500 psi compressive strength, and special-inspector observation on every residential foundation pour in Crittenden, Greene, Mississippi, Poinsett, Cross, Lee, Phillips, St. Francis, Craighead, Lawrence, and Randolph counties — the most stringent foundation-specific seismic mandate in the South Central region.
AR.climate_concern: Arkansas's New Madrid Seismic Zone influence across northeast Arkansas counties from Jonesboro to West Memphis forces ASCE 7-22 SDC C-D0 seismic design category requirements on every residential foundation pour in the corridor, and Arkansas's red-clay Delta subsoil across the eastern half of the state requires aggressive 4-to-6-inch crushed-stone capillary break under every slab pour, plus Ozark and Ouachita mountain pours additionally require frost-protected footing depth on every elevated residential construction site above 1,500 ft elevation where freeze-thaw exposure runs F1.

CA.name: California
CA.license_board_name: California Contractors State License Board — C-8 Concrete or C-50 Reinforcing Steel (foundation specialty)
CA.permit_typical: city building permit + engineered foundation drawings required on every residential new-construction pour; CSLB C-8 license required above $500 contract; mandatory City/County engineered drainage plan in coastal CA; geotechnical soils report required on every lot above 0.5 acre per CBC §1803
CA.distinctive_law: California Title 24 Part 9 (California Building Code) Chapter 18 plus ASCE 7-22 SDC D2 seismic design category requires the most aggressive residential foundation reinforcement scheme in the country — every California new-construction foundation requires continuous-rebar-tie schedules with minimum #4 rebar at 16-inch on-center, continuous bond beam reinforcement, hold-down anchors at every shear-wall corner, and a CALGreen-required Type IL or Type IS SCM-blended cement specification on every covered project, with CDPH-certified concrete admixture compliance reviewed on every Bay Area, Los Angeles, and San Diego pour.
CA.climate_concern: California's combination of ASCE 7-22 SDC D2 seismic design category across the entire state, expansive Bay Area and Central Valley clay subsoil, San Andreas Fault near-fault design requirements in the Coast Ranges, and Pacific salt-spray on every coastal exposure drives the most expensive residential foundation pour requirements in the country, and California's CAL FIRE Tier 3 WUI overlay across the Sierra foothills additionally requires Class A fire-rated concrete fascia and continuous concrete walkway separation on every WUI residential property to provide ember-resistant foundation perimeter protection.

CO.name: Colorado
CO.license_board_name: No statewide foundation license — Denver, Colorado Springs, Aurora issue municipal contractor credentials and require Class B General Contractor for residential foundation work
CO.permit_typical: city or county building permit + soils report required for every residential foundation pour above 5% slope per Colorado Soil Disclosure Act; Front Range subdivisions routinely require post-tension foundation specifications instead of standard slab-on-grade due to bentonite-driven slab heave; Colorado state radon zone 1 mandates sub-slab depressurization rough-in
CO.distinctive_law: Colorado Soil Disclosure Act (CRS §6-6.5) imposes the most restrictive expansive-soil disclosure regime in the country — every Colorado residential property seller must provide a written disclosure of swelling soil risk to every buyer, and every Colorado new-construction foundation pour must include a Colorado Geological Survey-recognized soils report identifying expansive bentonitic clay subsoil risk, plus Colorado Front Range subdivisions routinely require post-tension cable foundation specifications, structural floor (rather than slab-on-grade), or piered foundations with caissons drilled to bedrock on every Aurora, Castle Rock, Highlands Ranch, and Parker residential foundation due to bentonite-driven slab heave on the Denver Basin.
CO.climate_concern: Colorado's combination of expansive bentonitic clay subsoil across the Front Range and Eastern Plains, F2 freeze-thaw exposure across the state, and 36-inch frost depth forces aggressive expansive-soil mitigation on every Colorado residential foundation pour — Front Range subdivisions in Aurora, Castle Rock, Highlands Ranch, and Parker routinely use caisson-drilled-pier foundations with structural floors instead of slab-on-grade, and Colorado statewide radon zone 1 designation forces sub-slab depressurization rough-in pipes through every residential basement and slab pour to enable post-construction radon mitigation system installation.

CT.name: Connecticut
CT.license_board_name: Connecticut Department of Consumer Protection — Home Improvement Contractor (HIC) registration + New Home Construction Contractor (NHCC) for new builds
CT.permit_typical: city or town building permit required for residential foundation pour; HIC registration required above $200 contract; NHCC license required for new-home foundation; Connecticut Crumbling Foundation Law mandatory aggregate-source certification on every residential pour in 41 affected northeastern CT towns
CT.distinctive_law: Connecticut Crumbling Foundation Law (CT Gen. Stat. §16a-44a) is the only U.S. state law specifically addressing pyrrhotite-contaminated aggregate foundation failure — Connecticut Department of Housing has cataloged over 30,000 affected homes with crumbling foundations from pyrrhotite-bearing aggregate sourced from a single quarry between 1983-2015, and every modern Connecticut residential foundation pour now requires aggregate source certification under the Connecticut Crumbling Foundation Law plus mandatory testing on suspect-source aggregate, with the Connecticut Foundation Solutions Indemnity Company captive insurer reimbursing affected homeowners up to $190,000 per home for full foundation replacement.
CT.climate_concern: Connecticut's pyrrhotite-contaminated aggregate crisis affecting 41 northeastern Connecticut towns from Stafford Springs to Tolland is the most acute residential foundation crisis in the country — Connecticut Department of Housing has cataloged over 30,000 affected homes with crumbling foundations, with full foundation replacement averaging $150,000-$250,000 per home and structural cracking accelerating during freeze-thaw cycles when expansive iron sulfide oxidizes within the concrete matrix, and every modern Connecticut residential foundation pour now requires aggregate source certification under the Connecticut Crumbling Foundation Law to prevent further pyrrhotite-driven foundation failure.

DE.name: Delaware
DE.license_board_name: Delaware Division of Revenue — Business License (foundation contractor) + Delaware Division of Professional Regulation Building Contractor
DE.permit_typical: county building permit required for residential foundation pour above 200 sq ft; Delaware Business License only — no state-issued specialty foundation license; Delaware Coastal Zone Act §7001 stormwater notification required on every Sussex County coastal pour
DE.distinctive_law: Delaware is one of the few East Coast states with no specialty foundation or building contractor licensing — every Delaware foundation contractor operates under a Delaware Division of Revenue Business License only, with no examination, bond, or board oversight, leaving Sussex County coastal property owners to rely on contractor BBB ratings and Delaware Consumer Protection Unit complaint history rather than a state credential check, and Delaware Coastal Zone Act §7001 separately restricts foundation excavation stormwater runoff within the Inland Bays watershed across Sussex County requiring a permit and erosion-and-sediment plan on every Bethany Beach to Lewes residential pour.
DE.climate_concern: Delaware's combination of high water table across the Coastal Plain south of the C&D Canal, sandy fast-draining soils across the Cape Henlopen-to-Bethany corridor, and persistent Atlantic salt-spray on every Sussex County coastal exposure drives capillary-break crushed-stone specification under every coastal Delaware crawl-space pour, and Delaware Bay coastal flood zones plus the FEMA NFIP Special Flood Hazard Area mapping requires base-flood-elevation foundation height verification on every Bethany Beach to Rehoboth coastal residential foundation, with mandatory flood vents on every below-base-flood-elevation crawl space.

FL.name: Florida
FL.license_board_name: Florida Department of Business & Professional Regulation — Construction Industry Licensing Board (Certified Residential Contractor or Specialty Foundation Contractor)
FL.permit_typical: city or county building permit required for residential foundation pour; FL DBPR specialty license required statewide; HVHZ permit + Florida Product Approval (FL #) NOA verification mandatory in Miami-Dade and Broward; sinkhole disclosure required under FL Statute §501.140
FL.distinctive_law: Florida Building Code §1804 + §1810 imposes the most restrictive coastal hurricane-zone foundation requirements in the country — every HVHZ residential foundation pour in Miami-Dade and Broward counties requires Florida Product Approval (FL #) compliance with Notice of Acceptance (NOA) testing for hurricane wind uplift up to 175 mph, plus continuous epoxy-coated rebar tie schedules and minimum 4500 psi compressive strength on hurricane-zone foundations, and Florida Statute §501.140 (Sinkhole Disclosure) separately requires every Florida property seller to disclose any sinkhole activity on every residential property transfer with optional homeowner's-insurance Sinkhole Loss Coverage endorsement.
FL.climate_concern: Florida's combination of Atlantic and Gulf hurricane wind uplift requirements driving HVHZ Miami-Dade and Broward NOA-rated foundation tie-down schedules at 175 mph design wind, persistent year-round humidity driving rapid concrete set times requiring retarder admixtures on every pour, and Florida's high water table across the entire peninsula requiring waterproof admixtures plus capillary-break crushed-stone on every below-grade pour, plus Central Florida sinkhole-prone karst limestone requiring sinkhole-disclosure-and-investigation under FL Statute §501.140 on every residential transfer drives the most specialized residential foundation pour requirements in the Southeast.

GA.name: Georgia
GA.license_board_name: Georgia State Licensing Board for Residential and General Contractors — residential-basic or residential-light
GA.permit_typical: city or county building permit required for residential foundation pour on new construction; residential-basic or residential-light contractor license required above $2,500 contract; Georgia EPD Stormwater NOI required on every disturbed-acre residential subdivision pour
GA.distinctive_law: Georgia Code §43-41 created the State Licensing Board for Residential and General Contractors in 2008, making Georgia one of the most recent Southeast states to centralize residential contractor licensing — every Georgia foundation contractor exceeding the $2,500 contract threshold must hold a residential-basic or residential-light license, plus Georgia Code §44-3 separately enforces the Georgia Property Owners Association Act requiring HOA-approved foundation contractor selection on every covered residential property in deed-restricted Atlanta-area communities, and Georgia EPD Stormwater requires erosion-and-sediment plan plus NOI on every Atlanta subdivision lot foundation pour above 1 acre disturbed area.
GA.climate_concern: Georgia's red-clay Piedmont subsoil across the Atlanta-Athens-Macon corridor requires aggressive 4-to-6-inch crushed-stone capillary break under every slab pour to prevent clay-driven slab heave during seasonal moisture cycles, and Georgia's high mature-deciduous tree canopy across the Atlanta-Athens corridor combined with persistent root-driven slab uplift on existing foundations drives standard root-barrier and continuous expansion-joint installation on every Atlanta and Athens residential foundation, plus Georgia coastal Chatham, Bryan, Liberty, McIntosh, Glynn, and Camden counties require Atlantic hurricane wind tie-down detailing on every coastal residential foundation pour.

HI.name: Hawaii
HI.license_board_name: Hawaii Department of Commerce & Consumer Affairs — Contractors License Board (C-31 Cement Concrete + C-5 Cabinetry / Limited Specialty)
HI.permit_typical: county building permit required for residential foundation pour; HI DCCA C-31 specialty license required statewide; mandatory geotechnical investigation on every Big Island lava-flow zone residential pour; Volcanic Hazard Zone disclosure required under HRS §433H
HI.distinctive_law: Hawaii Revised Statutes §433H requires every Big Island residential property seller to disclose Volcanic Hazard Zone classification (1-9, with Zone 1 highest risk on the Mauna Loa southwest rift zone) on every transfer, and the County of Hawaii Building Division separately requires geotechnical investigation reports identifying lava-tube voids and pyroclastic-fall thickness on every Big Island residential foundation pour, with Hawaii County Code Chapter 5 separately requiring engineered foundation plans for any Mauna Loa or Kilauea zone-1 residential pour and the State of Hawaii ASCE 7-22 SDC D1 seismic design category driving aggressive rebar tie schedules on every island residential foundation.
HI.climate_concern: Hawaii's combination of ASCE 7-22 SDC D1 seismic design category statewide, Big Island volcanic hazard zone risk requiring lava-tube void detection on every Mauna Loa rift-zone residential pour, persistent Pacific salt-spray on every coastal exposure, and tropical hurricane wind exposure driving Hurricane-Iniki-revised tie-down requirements on every Kauai residential foundation pour drives aggressive corrosion-protected reinforcement plus engineered foundation plans on every Hawaii residential foundation, with Big Island Kona and Hilo subdivisions additionally requiring volcanic-ash-fall load review on every roof-foundation system.

ID.name: Idaho
ID.license_board_name: Idaho Bureau of Occupational Licenses — Public Works Contractor License (PWCL) for residential >$2,000 + Idaho Contractors Registration
ID.permit_typical: city or county building permit required for residential foundation pour above 200 sq ft; PWCL required for public + private residential work above $2,000; Treasure Valley clay subsoil requires geotechnical investigation; Idaho radon zone 1 mandates sub-slab depressurization rough-in
ID.distinctive_law: Idaho is one of only a few Mountain West states without a centralized residential builder license — Idaho Bureau of Occupational Licenses operates a Public Works Contractor License (PWCL) for residential foundation work above $2,000 plus a Contractors Registration system without examination or bond requirement, leaving Treasure Valley homeowners to rely on PWCL bond (only $25,000) and Idaho Contractor Registration history rather than a state-board credential check, plus Boise, Meridian, and Nampa municipalities separately enforce Treasure Valley expansive-clay geotechnical investigation requirements on every Ada and Canyon County residential foundation pour above 5,000 sq ft footprint.
ID.climate_concern: Idaho's combination of 36-inch frost depth across the Treasure Valley and Snake River Plain, expansive Treasure Valley clay subsoil across Ada and Canyon counties, and Idaho statewide radon zone 1 designation drives aggressive frost-protected footing depth, capillary-break crushed-stone, and sub-slab depressurization rough-in on every Boise-Meridian-Nampa residential foundation pour, with northern Idaho mountain pours additionally requiring frost-protected shallow foundation (FPSF) designs in alpine subdivisions where excavation to 36-inch frost depth is impractical.

IL.name: Illinois
IL.license_board_name: No statewide foundation contractor license — Cook County, City of Chicago, and most home-rule municipalities issue Building Contractor licenses
IL.permit_typical: city building permit + Cook County Stormwater Management permit required on every residential foundation pour; Illinois Plumbing Code mandatory backwater valve and sump-pump on every basement pour; Chicago radon-resistant new construction mandatory under City Ordinance 18-6-A
IL.distinctive_law: Illinois is one of the few Midwest states without a statewide residential builder license — Cook County, City of Chicago, and most home-rule municipalities issue municipal Building Contractor licenses but no statewide examination or bond is required, and Illinois Plumbing Code 77 IL Adm Code 890 separately mandates a backwater valve plus dual sump-pump installation on every Illinois residential basement pour to prevent combined-sewer backup flooding, with City of Chicago Ordinance 18-6-A additionally requiring radon-resistant new construction sub-slab depressurization rough-in on every Chicago residential basement and slab pour.
IL.climate_concern: Illinois's combination of 42-inch frost depth across Chicago and northern Illinois, F2 freeze-thaw exposure driving 5-7% air-entrainment specification, high Lake Michigan water table across Cook and Lake counties driving sump-pump plus dual backwater-valve installation on every basement pour, statewide radon zone 1 designation forcing sub-slab depressurization rough-in on every residential foundation, and southern Illinois New Madrid Seismic Zone influence in St. Clair, Madison, Monroe, Randolph, Jackson, Williamson, and Pulaski counties forcing ASCE 7-22 SDC C-D0 seismic detailing makes Illinois one of the most foundation-engineering-intensive states in the country.

IN.name: Indiana
IN.license_board_name: No statewide foundation contractor license — Marion County (Indianapolis), Hamilton County, and other home-rule municipalities issue local Builder Contractor credentials
IN.permit_typical: city or county building permit required for residential foundation pour; IN Indiana Building Code 675 IAC mandatory; statewide Indiana radon zone 1 designation across northern half drives sub-slab depressurization rough-in
IN.distinctive_law: Indiana is one of the few large states without a statewide residential builder license — Marion County, Hamilton County, and other Indiana home-rule municipalities issue local Builder Contractor credentials, but no centralized state board oversees foundation contractor competency or bond requirements, leaving Indianapolis and Indianapolis suburb homeowners to rely on municipal license history rather than a state-board examination check, plus Indiana Code §16-41-39 separately enforces statewide radon-resistant new construction in 39 northern Indiana counties (radon zone 1) requiring sub-slab depressurization rough-in on every residential basement and slab pour.
IN.climate_concern: Indiana's combination of 36-inch frost depth across northern Indiana, F2 freeze-thaw exposure driving 5-7% air-entrainment specification, and statewide radon zone 1 designation across the northern half (39 of 92 counties classified zone 1) drives sub-slab depressurization rough-in on every northern Indiana residential foundation pour, with the Indianapolis metro plus Hamilton, Hancock, Hendricks, Johnson, and Madison counties additionally requiring continuous footing reinforcement on every residential foundation pour to manage F2 freeze-thaw exposure across the Central Indiana glacial-till plain.

IA.name: Iowa
IA.license_board_name: Iowa Department of Workforce Development — Iowa Construction Contractor Registration
IA.permit_typical: city or county building permit required for residential foundation pour above 200 sq ft; Iowa Construction Contractor Registration required for foundation work above $2,000; Iowa loess-soil Council Bluffs and Sioux City foundations require geotechnical investigation; statewide radon zone 1 mandates sub-slab depressurization rough-in
IA.distinctive_law: Iowa Code §91C requires every Iowa foundation contractor to register with the Iowa Construction Contractor Registration program plus carry a $25,000 surety bond — making Iowa one of the few states without a centralized residential builder license but with a meaningful financial-security requirement, and Iowa Department of Public Health separately enforces statewide radon-resistant new construction on every residential foundation pour due to Iowa's national-leader radon-zone-1 designation across all 99 counties (Iowa has the highest residential radon levels of any U.S. state at 39% of homes above 4 pCi/L), making Iowa the most radon-driven foundation regulatory environment in the country.
IA.climate_concern: Iowa's national-leader radon exposure (39% of homes above 4 pCi/L action level, the highest residential radon prevalence of any U.S. state) drives mandatory sub-slab depressurization rough-in pipe specification on every Iowa residential foundation pour, and Iowa's combination of 48-inch frost depth, F2-F3 freeze-thaw exposure across the northern half, and Loess soil (windblown silt) across Council Bluffs, Sioux City, and the Loess Hills State Forest forces aggressive frost-protected footing depth, geotechnical investigation, and 5-7% air-entrainment specification on every Iowa residential foundation pour.

KS.name: Kansas
KS.license_board_name: Kansas Real Estate Commission Builder Disclosure + Local Building Department — no state foundation contractor license
KS.permit_typical: city or county building permit required for residential foundation pour above 200 sq ft; no statewide builder license but tornado-shelter rough-in mandatory under KS Building Code §1907 in tornado-prone counties
KS.distinctive_law: Kansas has no statewide residential builder license — local building departments issue municipal contractor credentials in Wichita, Overland Park, Kansas City, Topeka, and Lawrence, but no centralized state board oversees foundation contractor competency or bond requirements, and Kansas is unusual among Tornado Alley states in not enforcing a statewide tornado-shelter mandate, although individual Wichita, Overland Park, and Topeka municipalities separately require basement-or-storm-shelter rough-in detailing on every residential foundation pour, plus Kansas Department of Health and Environment statewide radon zone 1 designation drives sub-slab depressurization rough-in on every residential basement.
KS.climate_concern: Kansas's combination of 36-inch frost depth across the Flint Hills and Smoky Hills, F2 freeze-thaw exposure across the northern half, statewide tornado exposure driving optional storm-shelter rough-in detailing on every residential foundation pour, and statewide Kansas Department of Health radon zone 1 designation forcing sub-slab depressurization rough-in on every Kansas residential foundation drives a unique combination of seismic-quiet but tornado-and-radon-driven foundation reinforcement requirements, with eastern Kansas additionally requiring expansive-clay capillary-break aggregate beneath every Topeka and Kansas City residential pour.

KY.name: Kentucky
KY.license_board_name: Kentucky Department of Housing, Buildings & Construction — no statewide foundation contractor license but mandatory state-issued Builder License for residential general contractors above 1,500 sq ft new construction
KY.permit_typical: city or county building permit required for residential foundation pour; Kentucky karst-zone sinkhole disclosure required under KY Statute §324.360; statewide radon zone 1 mandates sub-slab depressurization rough-in
KY.distinctive_law: Kentucky Revised Statutes §324.360 requires every Kentucky property seller to disclose any known karst-zone sinkhole activity on every residential property transfer — Kentucky has the highest concentration of karst limestone in the eastern U.S. with sinkholes mapped across 38 of 120 Kentucky counties, plus Kentucky New Madrid Seismic Zone influence across western Kentucky counties (Hickman, Fulton, Carlisle, Ballard, McCracken, Graves) forces ASCE 7-22 SDC C-D0 seismic detailing on every western Kentucky residential foundation pour, and statewide Kentucky radon zone 1 designation drives sub-slab depressurization rough-in on every residential basement.
KY.climate_concern: Kentucky's karst limestone subsoil across 38 of 120 counties (the highest concentration of karst in the eastern U.S.) drives mandatory sinkhole disclosure under KY Statute §324.360 on every Kentucky residential property transfer, and Kentucky's combination of statewide radon zone 1 designation, New Madrid Seismic Zone influence across the western Jackson Purchase counties forcing ASCE 7-22 SDC C-D0 seismic detailing, and 24-inch frost depth across the Bluegrass Region drives aggressive geotechnical investigation, sub-slab depressurization rough-in, and continuous footing reinforcement on every Kentucky residential foundation pour.

LA.name: Louisiana
LA.license_board_name: Louisiana State Licensing Board for Contractors — Residential Building Contractor + Specialty Foundation
LA.permit_typical: city or parish building permit required for residential foundation pour; LSLBC Residential Building Contractor license required above $75,000 contract; Louisiana Uniform Construction Code (LUCC) mandatory with hurricane wind tie-down on every coastal foundation; FEMA NFIP base-flood-elevation foundation height required on every coastal-parish residential pour
LA.distinctive_law: Louisiana Uniform Construction Code (LUCC) Title 17 requires every Louisiana coastal residential foundation pour to comply with the International Residential Code (IRC) with Louisiana-specific hurricane wind tie-down amendments adopted post-Katrina — every coastal parish (Jefferson, Orleans, Plaquemines, St. Bernard, St. Tammany, Lafourche, Terrebonne, St. Mary, Iberia, Vermilion, Cameron, Calcasieu) requires hurricane-rated continuous load path tie-down detailing from foundation to roof, plus FEMA NFIP base-flood-elevation foundation height verification on every below-base-flood-elevation foundation, with the Louisiana State Licensing Board for Contractors mandating a Residential Building Contractor license above $75,000 contract value for any new-construction foundation work.
LA.climate_concern: Louisiana's combination of high water table across the entire Mississippi alluvium (water within 5 feet of grade across most of South Louisiana), FEMA NFIP Special Flood Hazard Area mapping requiring base-flood-elevation foundation height on every coastal residential pour, expansive Yazoo and Terrebonne clay subsoil driving aggressive pier-and-beam pile-supported foundation design instead of slab-on-grade, and Atlantic-Gulf hurricane wind exposure driving Louisiana Uniform Construction Code hurricane tie-down requirements on every coastal parish residential pour drives the most water-and-wind-driven foundation regulatory environment in the U.S. South.

ME.name: Maine
ME.license_board_name: Maine has no statewide residential builder license — Maine Office of Professional & Financial Regulation oversees specialty trades only
ME.permit_typical: town or municipal building permit required for residential foundation pour; no state foundation contractor license; Maine MUBEC adoption mandates IRC + IECC compliance; statewide radon zone 1 mandates sub-slab depressurization rough-in
ME.distinctive_law: Maine has no statewide residential builder license — Maine Office of Professional & Financial Regulation oversees only specialty trades like electrical, plumbing, and oil-burner installation, leaving foundation contractor competency and bond entirely to municipal building departments and homeowner due diligence, and Maine Uniform Building & Energy Code (MUBEC) adoption since 2010 separately mandates IRC + IECC 2021 compliance on every residential foundation pour, with Maine Department of Health & Human Services statewide radon zone 1 designation driving sub-slab depressurization rough-in on every Maine residential basement pour.
ME.climate_concern: Maine's combination of 60-inch frost depth across the northern half (Aroostook County and Penobscot County), F3 freeze-thaw exposure driving 6-8% air-entrainment specification, statewide radon zone 1 designation forcing sub-slab depressurization rough-in on every residential basement pour, and Maine coastal Atlantic salt-spray on every Cumberland, York, Lincoln, Knox, Waldo, Hancock, and Washington county exposure drives aggressive frost-protected footing depth (5-foot minimum in Aroostook), corrosion-protected reinforcement on every coastal pour, and continuous capillary-break crushed-stone on every Maine residential foundation.

MD.name: Maryland
MD.license_board_name: Maryland Home Improvement Commission (MHIC) — Home Improvement Contractor license + Maryland Department of Labor New Home Construction Sub-Contractor
MD.permit_typical: city or county building permit required for residential foundation pour; MHIC license required for any home-improvement work above $500; Maryland Critical Area Act review required on every Chesapeake Bay residential pour within 1,000 feet of mean tidal elevation
